Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (3): Installing It
Next up is actually installing Python — because if we want to learn Python, we kind of need Python on our machine first.
Seeing this post, you might go "WT... what! You wrote a whole post just for installing Python? Are you trying to pad out another day of the challenge?!"
Yep, you read that right, I'm totally padding this one out.
Okay no, that was just a joke...

Installing Python really doesn't look like much, but there's a small difference between Windows and macOS, and there are a few gotchas hiding in there. So I figured I'd give it its own post and walk through the install on both operating systems, hopefully smoothing out some of the bumps for anyone who wants to learn Python — especially if you're coming in as a pure JavaScript developer.
Why do we even need to install Python
At this point you might also be wondering: why do we need to install Python? Can't Python just run on its own?
The main reason is that our computer has no idea what "language" the code we write is. The computer only reads and understands
, so we need an interpreter to convert our Python code into instructions the computer can actually read. Think of the interpreter as a middleman — basically a translator.

For example: at some international event, we speak Chinese but the other side only understands Arabic. Usually there'll be a translator sitting between the two people, translating Chinese into Arabic and Arabic back into Chinese. The Python interpreter we're about to install works on the exact same idea:
Now you might be thinking — but when I write JavaScript I never install anything? Actually, you already did. It's the
. That's why we can run JavaScript directly in the browser.
(If you want to dig deeper into how JavaScript actually works, I'd suggest reading
First, head to the official Python website — there's a Downloads option up top. Hover over it and you can grab the Python installer:
Now open the installer to install Python. There's one small spot to watch out for here — something a senior dev mentioned before:

I always assumed it didn't matter how you installed Python on Windows, since Python's compatibility on Windows is better than stuff like Ruby. So I just installed it however. Then when I deployed Django to IIS I got burned hard — took me a whole day to find the fix. That's when I learned Python still has a few landmines.
So based on that senior dev's heads-up, there are two key points:
Don't make the install path too long
Don't put spaces in the path

So when installing, I'd suggest clicking "Customize installation", and checking "Add Python to PATH". That "Add Python to PATH" basically adds the Python command to your computer's environment variables — otherwise you won't be able to use it:
For Optional Features, basically check everything, then hit Next and you're good:
Next, check "Install for all users" — in short, this means we're installing Python so every user on this machine can use it. Then change the path below to "
" and you can start the install:
(If a permission prompt pops up, click "Yes".)
Once it's done you can hit the "Close" button, then open a terminal and type

But since we'll be using Python 3.9 as our learning version, we'll need to install Python 3.9 here. Sure, Mac can also go to the official Python website and download the installer:

But honestly, here I'd recommend using Homebrew instead. The main reason is that there are many ways to install apps, and the install paths end up scattered all over the place, which makes things a pain when you want to tweak or delete them later. So here I'd suggest using the package manager Homebrew to install Python:

So since we want to install Python 3, we can type this in the terminal:

to check it installed correctly. If you've learned Python before, you might wonder why pip (a package manager tool, kind of like npm) wasn't installed. Actually, pip comes built in from Python 3 onward, and to use pip here the command is
As for what pip is — we'll get into that later. For now just know it's a package manager tool, and we'll cover the details down the road.

I have to say, I really do recommend using Homebrew to install Python. At first I used the official installer, and later when I needed to delete Python for an article, I found it had installed to two different paths — and while deleting I was terrified of accidentally wiping out some other config file. Total nightmare.

For this series I'm using VSCode as the editor. Some people might use PyCharm — honestly, anything that lets you practice writing Python works, so go with whatever you're comfortable with. This series will mostly stick with VSCode.
